name: Array
explanation:
An array is a linear data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element can be accessed directly by its index, making lookup extremely fast. Arrays have a fixed size in most languages but dynamic arrays (like Kotlin's ArrayList or Java's ArrayList) can grow as needed.

Key characteristics:
• Contiguous memory allocation
• Zero-based indexing
• Homogeneous elements (same data type)
• Constant-time O(1) access by index
whenToUseJson: ["When you need fast indexed access to elements","When the data size is known or predictable in advance","When you need memory efficiency (no overhead per element)","When iterating through elements sequentially"]
codeExample:
fun main() {
    val arr = intArrayOf(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
    println(arr[0])

    val list = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
    list.add(4)
    list.removeAt(0)
    println(list)
}

name: Linked List
explanation:
A linked list is a linear data structure where elements (nodes) are stored non-contiguously, connected via pointers. Each node contains data and a reference to the next node.

Key characteristics:
• Dynamic size (grows/shrinks at runtime)
• No memory wastage (allocated per element)
• Sequential access only (no direct indexing)
• Efficient insertions/deletions at head
whenToUseJson: ["When you need frequent insertions/deletions at the beginning","When the data size changes frequently and unpredictably","When implementing stacks, queues, or adjacency lists for graphs","When memory fragmentation is a concern with arrays"]

name: Stack
explanation:
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. Elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) from the top only.

Key characteristics:
• LIFO ordering
• Operations: push (add), pop (remove), peek (view top)
• Can be implemented using arrays or linked lists
• Used extensively in function call stacks and expression evaluation
whenToUseJson: ["When you need LIFO behavior","For undo/redo functionality in editors","For parsing expressions","For backtracking algorithms (DFS, maze solving)","For function call management"]
codeExample:
fun main() {
    val stack = ArrayDeque<Int>()
    stack.addLast(1)
    stack.addLast(2)
    stack.addLast(3)
    println(stack.last())
    println(stack.removeLast())
    println(stack)
}

name: Queue
explanation:
A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue).

Variations include Circular Queue, Priority Queue, and Deque.
whenToUseJson: ["When you need FIFO behavior","For BFS in graphs","For task scheduling and job queues","For buffering data streams","For handling requests in order"]
codeExample:
fun main() {
    val queue = ArrayDeque<Int>()
    queue.addLast(1)
    queue.addLast(2)
    queue.addLast(3)
    println(queue.first())
    println(queue.removeFirst())
    println(queue)
}

name: Binary Tree
explanation:
A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children. The topmost node is called the root.

Key characteristics:
• Each node has at most 2 children
• Strictly hierarchical (no cycles)
• Depth = number of edges from root to node
• Height = max depth of any node
whenToUseJson: ["When data has a hierarchical relationship","For representing file systems, organization charts","For expression trees","As the foundation for BST, AVL, and Heap structures","For Huffman encoding"]

name: Binary Search Tree
explanation:
A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree with a special ordering property: for every node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are greater.

Key characteristics:
• Left subtree contains values < node
• Right subtree contains values > node
• No duplicate values (typically)
• In-order traversal yields sorted order
whenToUseJson: ["When you need fast search, insert, and delete operations","When you need to maintain a sorted collection dynamically","For implementing maps and sets","For solving range query problems"]

name: AVL Tree
explanation:
An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where the height difference (balance factor) between left and right subtrees of any node is at most 1.

Balance factor = height(left) - height(right). Allowed values: -1, 0, 1.
whenToUseJson: ["When you need guaranteed O(log n) search performance","When insertion order is unpredictable","For database indexing systems","For memory-constrained systems where worst-case matters"]
codeExample:
class AVLNode(val value: Int) {
    var left: AVLNode? = null
    var right: AVLNode? = null
    var height: Int = 1

    fun balanceFactor(): Int =
        (left?.height ?: 0) - (right?.height ?: 0)
}

name: Heap
explanation:
A heap is a specialized complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property. In a Max-Heap, the parent is always >= children. In a Min-Heap, the parent is always <= children.

Key characteristics:
• Complete binary tree
• Heap property
• Typically implemented using arrays
• Insert and extract are O(log n)
whenToUseJson: ["For implementing priority queues","For finding the k-th largest/smallest element","For heap sort","For scheduling algorithms","For streaming median calculation"]
codeExample:
fun main() {
    val minHeap = PriorityQueue<Int>()
    minHeap.add(5); minHeap.add(3); minHeap.add(7)
    println(minHeap.poll())

    val maxHeap = PriorityQueue<Int>(reverseOrder())
    maxHeap.add(5); maxHeap.add(3); maxHeap.add(7)
    println(maxHeap.poll())
}

name: Trie (Prefix Tree)
explanation:
A trie is a tree-like data structure that stores strings by splitting them into characters. Each node represents a single character, and paths from root to leaf nodes form complete words.

Key characteristics:
• Each node represents one character
• Root represents empty string
• Nodes can have up to 26 children (for English)
• Also called a digital tree or radix tree
whenToUseJson: ["For autocomplete and search suggestions","For spell checking and dictionary implementations","For IP routing (longest prefix matching)","For solving word games","For text pattern matching"]

name: Graph
explanation:
A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between nodes). Graphs model pairwise relationships.

Key representations:
• Adjacency Matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup
• Adjacency List: O(V+E) space, O(degree(v)) edge lookup
whenToUseJson: ["For modeling networks","For finding shortest paths","For dependency resolution","For web crawling","For recommendation systems"]

name: Weighted Graph
explanation:
A weighted graph extends the standard graph by assigning a numerical weight to each edge, representing cost, distance, capacity, or any other metric.

Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source to all vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights.
whenToUseJson: ["For GPS navigation (shortest path)","For network routing","For airline route planning","For project scheduling","For optimization problems with costs"]

name: Graph Algorithms
explanation: Beyond BFS and DFS, several fundamental graph algorithms: Topological Sort, Minimum Spanning Tree, Strongly Connected Components, Union-Find.
complexityJson: {"Topological Sort":"O(V + E)","MST (Kruskal's)":"O(E log E)","SCC (Kosaraju)":"O(V + E)","Cycle Detection":"O(V + E)"}
whenToUseJson: ["For task scheduling with dependencies","For designing minimum-cost networks","For detecting cycles in dependencies","For finding connected clusters","For solving dynamic connectivity problems"]

name: Hash Table
explanation:
A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function, which computes an index into an array of buckets.

Collision resolution: Separate Chaining (linked list per bucket) or Open Addressing (probing).

Average-case O(1) for all operations.
whenToUseJson: ["When you need fast key-value lookups","For implementing caches and memoization","For counting frequencies","For deduplication","For database indexing"]

name: Hash Set
explanation: A hash set is a collection of unique elements implemented using a hash table. It provides O(1) average-time add, remove, and contains operations.
whenToUseJson: ["When you need to ensure element uniqueness","For fast membership testing","For removing duplicates","For mathematical set operations","For tracking visited nodes"]
codeExample:
fun main() {
    val set = mutableSetOf(1, 2, 3, 3, 4)
    println(set)
    set.add(5)
    println(3 in set)
    println(10 in set)
    val setA = setOf(1, 2, 3, 4)
    val setB = setOf(3, 4, 5, 6)
    println(setA intersect setB)
    println(setA union setB)
}

name: Disjoint Set (Union-Find)
explanation:
A Disjoint Set tracks a partition of a set into disjoint subsets. It supports find (which set does an element belong to?) and union (merge two sets).

Optimizations: Path Compression and Union by Rank make operations nearly O(1).
whenToUseJson: ["For tracking connected components","For Kruskal's MST algorithm","For detecting cycles in undirected graphs","For social network analysis","For image segmentation"]

name: Segment Tree
explanation:
A segment tree is a binary tree for storing intervals or segments. It supports range queries (sum, min, max) with point updates in O(log n) time.

Key characteristics:
• 4N space for an array of size N
• Built recursively
• Range query and point update: O(log n)
complexityJson: {"Build":"O(n)","Range Query":"O(log n)","Point Update":"O(log n)","Range Update (lazy)":"O(log n)","Space":"O(n)"}
whenToUseJson: ["For range sum/min/max queries with updates","For competitive programming range queries","For prefix sums with modifications","For interval scheduling","For computational geometry"]
